title: Silk
summary: The lustrous textile produced from silkworm cocoons — China's most consequential technological secret, the commodity that named the most important trade route in pre-modern history, and a material whose production, trade, and political symbolism reorganized economies and societies from East Asia to the Mediterranean for three thousand years.
hook: For a thousand years, China executed anyone who revealed the secret of silk production. It was the most protected industrial secret in history. Eventually the secret escaped — through Byzantium, through monks smuggling silkworm eggs, through the Islamic world — and the technology spread. But by then, silk had already reorganized the world.

beginner.theFoundation:
Silk is a natural fiber produced by silkworms when they spin their cocoons. A single cocoon can yield up to a kilometer of continuous thread. When woven, that thread produces fabric with unique properties: lustrous, strong, light, and able to absorb dye brilliantly.

Silk was discovered in China, probably around 2700 BCE. For most of the next three thousand years, China was the world's only producer. The secret of sericulture — the cultivation of silkworms and the reeling of their thread — was guarded with the severity that modern states reserve for weapons technology. The trade routes that carried silk westward are what we now call the Silk Road. Along with silk traveled ideas, religions, technologies, and diseases.
beginner.howItArrived:
The Chinese state recognized the strategic value of the silk monopoly early. Export of silkworms or their eggs was forbidden on pain of death. The technology — the mulberry trees that fed the silkworms, the techniques of cocoon-reeling and thread-spinning — was protected as a state secret for roughly two thousand years.

The secret escaped in the sixth century CE, when Byzantine monks smuggled silkworm eggs out of China hidden in hollow walking staffs. From Byzantium, sericulture spread to Persia, then to the Islamic world, then to medieval Italy. China's monopoly was broken — but by then silk had already shaped the world.
beginner.whatItReorganized:
Silk reorganized the economies of every society it touched. In China, taxes were paid in silk, armies were paid in silk, and diplomatic gifts consisted of silk. The imperial workshops produced silk for court use and for the tributary system through which China managed relationships with neighboring states.

Along the Silk Road, the trade in silk supported a network of oasis cities — Samarkand, Kashgar, Dunhuang — whose prosperity depended on merchant passage.

In Rome and Byzantium, silk was a luxury so expensive that emperors issued laws limiting who could wear it. Silk imports drained the Roman balance of trade in ways Rome's own exports could not offset. It was expensive enough to be used as currency.
beginner.thePoliticalEconomy:
Luxury goods have specific political properties: they can be controlled, taxed, and used diplomatically in ways that bulk commodities cannot. China used silk systematically as a diplomatic instrument — giving it to neighboring rulers, demanding it in return for trading privileges, treating production capacity as a measure of civilization.

When the Islamic world absorbed silk production after the seventh century — building on Persian sericulture — the global geography of silk shifted. By the medieval period, the finest silk came from Iran and Iraq. The Italian city-states — Venice, Florence, Lucca — eventually became European centers of silk production, using technology ultimately derived from the Chinese original but transformed through centuries of transmission.
beginner.theFeedback:
Silk drove technological innovation that outlasted the fabric itself. The drawloom — a complex device for weaving patterned silk — was one of the most sophisticated mechanical devices of the pre-industrial world. It spread from China westward along the same routes as the silk, transforming textile production across Eurasia.

The Jacquard loom, which mechanized the drawloom's pattern-selection mechanism in 1804 using punched cards, became a direct ancestor of nineteenth-century computing systems. The conceptual link between silk weaving and computing runs through the same punch-card logic that Charles Babbage borrowed for his Analytical Engine.
beginner.presentAndFuture:
Silk production today is a fraction of its historical significance. Synthetic fibers — particularly nylon, developed partly as a synthetic silk substitute — have displaced it in most applications. Global production is dominated by China and India.

But the historical prestige of silk has not diminished. China's Belt and Road Initiative explicitly invokes the Silk Road as a precedent for Chinese-led Eurasian connectivity. The name is deliberate: it claims three thousand years of Chinese commercial reach for a contemporary infrastructure project.

general.theFoundation:
Silk's importance as a material foundation rests on a combination of properties that no other commodity quite replicated. It was valuable enough to serve as currency and diplomatic gift, light and durable enough to travel across vast distances, and technically complex enough to resist duplication for two millennia. No other commodity was immediately recognizable as Chinese in the same way. This combination made it uniquely suited to serve as the primary medium of Eurasian long-distance exchange.

Dieter Kuhn's work on Chinese silk production — particularly his contributions to the Science and Civilisation in China series — is the foundational scholarly account of the technical history. Xinru Liu's work on the Silk Road trades has established the economic and cultural dimensions of silk exchange. Together, they provide the framework within which silk's history as a material foundation can be understood.
general.howItArrived:
The question of how silk technology spread is one of the most studied problems in the history of technology transfer. The Byzantine acquisition — through monks who smuggled silkworm eggs from China in the 540s CE, according to Procopius — is the best-documented episode, though its accuracy has been questioned. What is clear is that sericulture reached Persia and Byzantium by the sixth century, the Islamic world by the seventh, and Italy by the twelfth.

Each transfer involved adaptation as well as adoption. The mulberry species favored in different regions, the silkworm varieties developed through selective breeding, and the weaving techniques evolved differently in each cultural context. Italian silk — particularly the luxurious velvets and brocades produced in Lucca and later Florence and Venice — represented a genuine technological and aesthetic development rather than simple imitation of Chinese originals.
general.whatItReorganized:
The Sogdian merchant letters — a cache of documents discovered near Dunhuang, dating to the early fourth century CE — are the most direct evidence for how the silk trade actually worked. They document commercial networks stretching from China to the Mediterranean, recording prices, credit arrangements, and partnerships in terms immediately recognizable to a modern reader.

The Sogdians' commercial dominance of Silk Road trade from roughly the fourth to the eighth century CE represents one of the most successful examples of a small people achieving outsize economic influence through commercial specialization. Their eventual eclipse — by the Arab and then Mongol reorganization of Central Asian politics — illustrates the dependence of commercial networks on the political stability they require.
general.thePoliticalEconomy:
The tributary system through which China managed its relationship with neighboring states was built in significant part on silk. Chinese emperors gave silk to steppe rulers — the Xiongnu, the Turks, the Mongols — as a combination of tribute, bribe, and diplomatic gift. The quantities were enormous: Han dynasty records document millions of bolts of silk flowing outward to steppe confederacies. This was not weakness but strategy: silk was cheaper than war, and its production was inexhaustible in ways that the steppe's military capacity was not.

The Islamic world's adoption of silk production transformed the commodity's political economy. When the caliphate controlled silk production in Persia and Iraq as well as the overland routes to China, it controlled both supply and the primary transit routes. This commercial dominance generated enormous revenue and supported the cultural flowering of the Abbasid period.
general.theFeedback:
The Jacquard loom's connection to computing is not incidental. Joseph-Marie Jacquard's 1804 device used punched cards to control the pattern of woven silk — a binary system in which the presence or absence of a hole determined whether a thread was raised or lowered. Charles Babbage saw Jacquard's loom operating in the 1820s and acknowledged it as an inspiration for his Analytical Engine.

Ada Lovelace described the Analytical Engine as weaving algebraic patterns just as the Jacquard loom weaved flowers and leaves. The thread from Chinese silk technology to modern computing, while long, is traceable. The drawloom that spread from China westward along the Silk Road routes became the mechanical precedent for the information processing systems of the industrial era.
general.presentAndFuture:
The Belt and Road Initiative's invocation of the Silk Road is historically interesting but also historically selective. The original Silk Roads were not Chinese state projects but emerged from the commercial activity of merchants — primarily Sogdian, then Arab, then Mongol — operating across political boundaries. They were sustained not by any single state's investment but by the commercial logic of luxury goods trade. The historical analogy is useful for legitimation but imprecise as description.

What the historical Silk Road and the Belt and Road Initiative share is the basic logic of infrastructure enabling connectivity enabling trade enabling political influence. The mechanism is the same; the scale, the state involvement, and the geopolitical context are entirely different. China's claim to three thousand years of commercial reach for a contemporary infrastructure project is selective history — but selective history with genuine historical grounding.

advanced.theFoundation:
The historiographical challenge of silk is the challenge of any commodity history: how to trace the full consequences of a material through the multiple societies it touched, across three thousand years, without either reducing everything to the commodity or losing the commodity in the complexity of the societies. Kenneth Pomeranz and Steven Topik's The World That Trade Created provides the methodological model: commodity histories as windows into the social and political arrangements that produce and consume them.

For silk specifically, the challenge is acute because the history spans so many linguistic traditions — Chinese, Persian, Arabic, Greek, Latin, Italian — that no single scholar commands them all. The synthetic accounts that exist (Xinru Liu's The Silk Road in World History, Valerie Hansen's The Silk Road: A New History) necessarily rely on scholars working in specific traditions, and the translations between them sometimes obscure as much as they illuminate.
advanced.howItArrived: The technology transfer question has been complicated by recent archaeological work. The standard narrative — Chinese monopoly broken by Byzantine monks in the 540s — has been challenged by evidence of sericulture in Sogdia and Persia before the Byzantine acquisition, suggesting that the technology spread through multiple channels and at earlier dates than the Procopius account implies. The silk fragments recovered from archaeological sites along the Silk Road, subjected to isotope analysis to determine their geographic origin, have revealed a more complex picture than the literary sources suggest: Chinese and non-Chinese silks were circulating simultaneously in many contexts.
advanced.whatItReorganized:
The Italian silk industry's development from the twelfth century onward represents the most thoroughly documented case of silk technology diffusion and transformation. The industry was established in Lucca with technology derived from Byzantine and Islamic sources; it spread to Florence, Venice, Genoa, and Milan as political disruptions drove craftsmen from city to city. The Florentine silk industry — which became the basis of the Medici commercial empire — drew on sophisticated credit and commercial institutions that were themselves partly developed to manage the risks of long-distance silk trade.

Giorgio Riello's work on the global history of cotton and silk has been important in contextualizing the Italian silk industry within the broader history of Eurasian textile exchange. His argument that textile history is a window into the history of globalization — that the movement of textile technologies and commodities is one of the primary mechanisms through which the pre-modern world was integrated — provides the analytical frame within which Italian silk development makes most sense.
advanced.thePoliticalEconomy:
The sumptuary regulations that governed silk consumption in Rome, Byzantium, the Islamic world, and medieval Europe reveal the political dimensions of luxury in a way that economic history alone cannot. Silk was not simply expensive — it was symbolically charged in ways that made its consumption a statement of social position. The regulations that tried to confine silk consumption to the elite were repeatedly violated and repeatedly renewed, which itself demonstrates both the desirability of silk and the difficulty of controlling consumption through law.

The Byzantine purple — the silk dyed with Tyrian purple that was reserved for the imperial family and its immediate circle — represents the extreme case of silk as political symbol. Imperial purple silk could not be purchased; it was given as a mark of imperial favor. The color and material together constituted a political language that was immediately readable across the Mediterranean world.
advanced.theFeedback:
The relationship between silk production and gender is one of the least studied but most consequential dimensions of silk's social history. In China, sericulture was traditionally women's work — the counterpart to men's agriculture in the classic formulation of the household economy. Women raised silkworms, reeled cocoons, and wove silk, while men farmed. This division of labor was ideologically encoded: the Confucian political economy rested on the complementarity of male agriculture and female sericulture.

In Europe, the silk industry was organized differently — with guild structures that were primarily male in weaving, female in some preparatory stages — but similarly depended on intensive female labor in ways that shaped the organization of households and workshops. The gendered dimensions of silk production have been studied most thoroughly for the Italian and French industries by historians of early modern Europe.
advanced.presentAndFuture:
The contemporary silk industry's concentration in China and India reproduces, in attenuated form, the historical pattern: China remains the dominant producer, with a market share of approximately 70-80% of global raw silk output. The labor intensity of sericulture — which resisted mechanization until the twentieth century — means that silk production has always been concentrated where low-cost female rural labor is available, which explains both the historical geography and the contemporary one.

The ecological dimensions of silk production are increasingly relevant: sericulture is environmentally benign relative to synthetic fiber production (which requires petroleum-derived chemicals) but requires monoculture mulberry cultivation that can have landscape effects. Research into the genetic engineering of silkworms — to produce silk with altered properties, including silk with incorporated proteins for biomedical applications — represents the most dynamic frontier of contemporary silk technology.

foundation.theStory: Silk is the commodity that named the most important trade route in pre-modern history and defined Chinese economic and diplomatic power for two thousand years. Its history is the history of how a single material — produced by a caterpillar's cocoon — reorganized economies, sustained empires, connected civilizations, and eventually contributed, through the Jacquard loom, to the invention of computing. Silk is the longest thread in the history of globalization.
foundation.theDebate: The central scholarly debates concern the mechanisms and timing of silk technology transfer (the Byzantine monk story is well-known but historically contested); the role of the Silk Road in transmitting not just silk but ideas, religions, and diseases; the gendered dimensions of silk production across different cultural contexts; and the relationship between silk's luxury status and its use as a diplomatic and political instrument.
foundation.whyItMatters: Silk matters as the primary commodity of pre-modern Eurasian exchange — the thread through which the connected world of the Silk Road was woven. It matters as a case study in the political economy of technological secrecy and its eventual failure. It matters as the material that drove the development of the Jacquard loom and, through it, the conceptual foundations of computing. And it matters as a window into the gendered organization of production in agricultural societies across Eurasia.
questions: China maintained the silk production monopoly for roughly two thousand years through state enforcement. What does this tell us about the relationship between technological secrets and state power? What eventually broke the monopoly, and what does that tell us about the limits of state control of technology?

The Silk Road was not a single road but a network of routes connecting many different political systems. What political conditions were necessary for long-distance silk trade to function? What happened to the trade when those conditions changed?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Silk scholarship divides between technical historians of production (Dieter Kuhn on Chinese sericulture; Giorgio Riello on the global textile history), historians of the Silk Road trades (Xinru Liu; Valerie Hansen; Étienne de la Vaissière on the Sogdians), and art historians who have studied the material culture of silk (Patricia Frick and others on Byzantine and Islamic silk). The challenge is integration: the production history, the trade history, and the material culture history are often pursued separately. Valerie Hansen's The Silk Road: A New History (2012), based on documents and artifacts found at oasis sites, represents the most successful recent integration.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for silk history is unusually rich in material terms — silk preserves well in dry conditions, and archaeological finds from Dunhuang to the Egyptian desert have produced substantial physical evidence of silk production, trade, and consumption. The Sogdian merchant letters provide direct evidence of commercial networks. The limits: the technology transfer question relies on literary sources (Procopius on the Byzantine acquisition) that may not be accurate; the gendered dimensions of silk production are documented unevenly across cultural contexts; and the experiences of the actual producers — peasant women reeling cocoons — are almost entirely invisible in the documentary record.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about silk must engage with both the material history (how silk was produced and what physical properties made it valuable) and the social history (how it was traded, regulated, consumed, and symbolically charged). Arguments that focus only on the trade route miss the production story; arguments that focus only on China miss the global story. The most productive frame treats silk as a connective material — a substance whose properties made it uniquely suited to serve as the medium of Eurasian integration.
questions: The Jacquard loom's punched-card system was acknowledged by Babbage and Lovelace as an inspiration for the Analytical Engine. What does this connection between textile technology and computing tell us about how technological innovation actually works — about the unexpected pathways through which one technology generates another?

Silk was used as currency, as a diplomatic gift, and as a marker of social status across multiple Eurasian civilizations. What properties of silk — physical, economic, symbolic — made it suited to these multiple functions? Are there contemporary commodities that play analogous roles?

research.topic: The Chinese origin and early history of silk production
research.content: Archaeological and textual evidence has established that silk production began in China by at least 3500 BCE, with domesticated silkworms and sophisticated weaving techniques present by the Shang dynasty. Dieter Kuhn's work in the Science and Civilisation in China series is the authoritative technical account. The physical evidence — silk threads, woven fragments, and sericulture tools from excavated sites — has been substantially augmented by isotope analysis, which allows determination of geographic origin for silk fragments found along the Silk Road.

research.topic: The timing and mechanisms of silk technology transfer
research.content: The standard account — Chinese monopoly maintained until the 540s CE Byzantine acquisition — has been complicated by archaeological evidence suggesting earlier and more complex technology diffusion. Isotope analysis of silk fragments from Central Asian and Western sites, combined with archaeological evidence of sericulture tools, suggests that silk production was underway in Sogdia and possibly Persia before the Byzantine acquisition. The full picture of how the technology spread, through what channels, and at what dates, remains an active area of research.

research.topic: The experience of silk producers — particularly the women who performed most of the labor
research.content: The overwhelming majority of silk production labor across Chinese history was performed by rural women — reeling cocoons, spinning thread, weaving cloth. Their experience of this labor, their economic situation, and their relationship to the silk they produced cannot be recovered in detail. What evidence exists: tax records and administrative documents that quantify silk output; literary texts that idealize sericulture as the feminine counterpart to male agriculture; occasional ethnographic accounts. What cannot be recovered: the day-to-day experience of silk production, the economic bargaining position of producing households, and the perspectives of the millions of women whose labor sustained the silk economy.

comparativeNarrative.name: Chinese Imperial Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: Within the Chinese tradition, silk was simultaneously an economic foundation, a political instrument, and a cultural marker of civilization. The association of silk with civilized life — versus the wool and leather of the nomadic peoples on China's borders — was ideologically central to Chinese self-understanding. The tributary system, through which silk flowed outward and prestige flowed inward, was a deliberate strategy for managing the steppe without constant military confrontation. The imperial workshops that produced silk for court use and diplomatic distribution were among the most sophisticated manufacturing operations of the pre-modern world.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Silk Road merchants — particularly the Sogdians
comparativeNarrative.name: Merchant and Intermediary Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: For the Sogdian merchants who dominated Silk Road trade from the fourth to the eighth century, silk was not a cultural symbol but a commercial commodity whose properties — high value, light weight, durable, easily standardized — made it ideal for long-distance trade. The Sogdian letters from Dunhuang reveal a world of pragmatic commercial calculation: prices, credit terms, partnership arrangements, and the risks of banditry and political disruption. The merchants who sustained silk trade were not ambassadors of cultural exchange but businesspeople managing complex commercial networks across political boundaries.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: European luxury consumers — medieval and early modern
comparativeNarrative.name: European Consumer Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: In medieval and early modern Europe, silk was the paramount luxury — the material in which kings were buried, in which saints were depicted, and which the sumptuary laws tried to confine to the elite. The European consumer's perspective on silk was shaped by its scarcity, its distance from its origins, and its association with the exotic East. The development of the Italian silk industry from the twelfth century onward brought production closer — reducing the distance between consumer and producer — but the symbolic association of silk with the Orient persisted, and was commercially exploited by Italian merchants who maintained the mystique of an Eastern luxury even as they manufactured it in Lucca and Florence.

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: Silk named the Silk Road and was its primary commodity — the two histories are inseparable.

rabbitHole.label: The Mongol Conquests
rabbitHole.reason: The Mongol conquests created the conditions for the Silk Road's greatest flourishing — the Pax Mongolica made overland Eurasian trade, and the silk that dominated it, safer than ever before.

rabbitHole.label: Cotton
rabbitHole.reason: Cotton and silk are the two great textile foundations of pre-modern civilization — silk for luxury, cotton for the mass market. Their histories illuminate each other.

rabbitHole.label: The Indian Ocean Trade
rabbitHole.reason: The maritime Silk Road — the sea routes connecting China, Southeast Asia, India, and the Persian Gulf — carried silk alongside spices, ceramics, and other luxury goods.

rabbitHole.label: China
rabbitHole.reason: Silk was China's most consequential export and a primary source of the state's diplomatic and economic power for two thousand years.

rabbitHole.label: Writing
rabbitHole.reason: Like writing, silk was a technology that spread from its origin culture to reorganize every civilization that adopted it — a parallel case of how a single innovation can reshape the world.

reference.author: Liu, Xinru
reference.title: The Silk Road in World History
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly synthesis of silk's role in world history — Liu traces the commodity from its Chinese origins through the multiple civilizations that traded, produced, and consumed it, with particular attention to the cultural and religious dimensions of exchange.

reference.author: Hansen, Valerie
reference.title: The Silk Road: A New History
reference.annotation: The most readable recent account — Hansen's approach through documents and artifacts found at oasis sites gives the history an immediacy that synthetic accounts lack. The essential starting point for general readers.

reference.author: Kuhn, Dieter
reference.title: Science and Civilisation in China, Vol. 5, Part 9: Textile Technology: Weaving and Rearing of Silk
reference.annotation: The authoritative technical history of Chinese silk production — Kuhn's contribution to the Needham series is the definitive account of the technology of sericulture and silk weaving from origins through the imperial period.

reference.author: de la Vaissière, Étienne
reference.title: Sogdian Traders: A History
reference.annotation: The essential account of the Sogdian merchants who dominated Silk Road trade — de la Vaissière's history of the Sogdians is the necessary complement to accounts focused on the commodity itself, showing who actually moved silk and how.

reference.author: Riello, Giorgio
reference.title: Cotton: The Fabric That Made the Modern World
reference.annotation: The essential comparative context — Riello's commodity history of cotton provides the framework for understanding silk's place in the global history of textile exchange, and his methodological approach to commodity history is exemplary.

reference.author: Larner, John
reference.title: Marco Polo and the Discovery of the World
reference.annotation: The essential account of the European encounter with Silk Road China — Larner's study of Marco Polo and his legacy shows how the silk trade shaped European imagination of the East and contributed to the impulse toward direct sea routes.

commerce.title: The Silk Road: A New History
commerce.author: Valerie Hansen
commerce.note: The best starting point — Hansen's account through oasis site documents is engaging, accessible, and genuinely new in its evidence base.

commerce.title: Silk Road: Monks, Warriors and Merchants
commerce.author: Luce Boulnois
commerce.note: A readable narrative account of the Silk Road in its broadest sense — Boulnois covers the full range of what the routes carried, with silk at the center.

commerce.title: The Silk Road in World History
commerce.author: Xinru Liu
commerce.note: The essential intermediate account — Liu traces silk from Chinese origins through multiple civilizations with particular attention to cultural and religious exchange alongside the commercial.

commerce.title: Sogdian Traders: A History
commerce.author: Étienne de la Vaissière
commerce.note: Essential for understanding who actually moved silk — de la Vaissière's account of the Sogdian merchants is the best available treatment of the commercial infrastructure of Silk Road trade.

commerce.title: Cotton: The Fabric That Made the Modern World
commerce.author: Giorgio Riello
commerce.note: Essential comparative context — Riello's commodity history of cotton provides the methodological model and the comparative frame for understanding silk's place in global textile history.

commerce.title: The Silk Road: A History
commerce.author: Xinru Liu and Lynda Norene Shaffer
commerce.note: The most comprehensive scholarly account — for readers who want the full depth of the scholarly literature on silk and Silk Road exchange.

popularCulture.title: The Belt and Road Initiative and Silk Road nostalgia
popularCulture.description: China's Belt and Road Initiative — its trillion-dollar infrastructure investment program — explicitly brands itself as a revival of the ancient Silk Road. The name works because the Silk Road carries connotations of connectivity, prosperity, and Chinese centrality that serve the project's political purposes. The historical analogy is imprecise (the original Silk Roads were merchant-driven, not state-planned), but the branding has been effective at framing the initiative as restoration rather than expansion.

popularCulture.title: Silk as a metaphor — 'the thread of history'
popularCulture.description: Silk's physical property — a continuous thread that can be traced from cocoon to garment — has made it irresistible as a metaphor for historical continuity and connection. The Silk Road 'connecting East and West' is one of the most durable clichés in world history writing. The metaphor is productive but also misleading: the historical Silk Roads were discontinuous, interrupted, and mediated by multiple intermediaries rather than a single continuous thread.

popularCulture.title: Silk Road exhibitions at major museums
popularCulture.description: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, the British Museum, and the Palace Museum in Beijing have all hosted major exhibitions on Silk Road exchange, consistently drawing large audiences. These exhibitions typically center on the luxury goods — silks, ceramics, metalwork, jewelry — that moved along the routes, making the abstract history of exchange concrete through objects. They are among the most effective popular introductions to the material culture of pre-modern globalization.

popularCulture.title: The Pillars of the Earth — Ken Follett (1989)
popularCulture.description: Follett's medieval epic is set in twelfth-century England, where silk from the Italian cities — itself derived from Byzantine and Islamic technology, ultimately from China — was among the most coveted luxury goods. The novel's texture of material life, including the role of luxury textiles in medieval social hierarchy, captures something real about how silk functioned in European society before it was produced locally.

popularCulture.title: Silk and computing — the Jacquard loom connection
popularCulture.description: The connection between the Jacquard loom's punched-card system and the development of computing is one of technology history's most surprising threads. Babbage saw the loom and was inspired; Lovelace described the Analytical Engine as weaving algebraic patterns as the loom wove silk flowers. The connection is cited in almost every history of computing and is taught in computer science courses as an example of how mechanical and computational ideas cross unexpected boundaries.
